confidence.disclosure: Sultan Suleiman I, called the Magnificent in the West and the Lawgiver (Kanuni) in Ottoman tradition, is among the best documented rulers of the early modern world. The Ottoman chroniclers (Celalzade, Nasuhi, Pecevi), the imperial archives, the diplomatic dispatches of Venetian baili, and the letters of Suleiman himself give us firm dates for almost every major event of his reign - his accession in 1520, the conquest of Belgrade in 1521, Rhodes in 1522, the battle of Mohacs in 1526, the siege of Vienna in 1529, the eastern campaigns against the Safavids, and his death at Szigetvar in 1566.

sources: Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age (1973)
Kaya Sahin, Empire and Power in the Reign of Süleyman (2013)

journey.event: Born on 6 November 1494 in Trabzon (Trebizond) on the Black Sea coast, where his father Selim was then provincial governor. His mother Hafsa Hatun is most likely a slave concubine. He is the only surviving son of Selim I.

Around the age of 14 is sent to Kefe (Caffa) in the Crimea as governor (sancak beg), the customary training post for Ottoman princes. His grandfather Bayezid II is then sultan, and Selim is governor of Trabzon.

After his father Selim I forces the abdication of Bayezid II and takes the throne in 1512, Suleiman is moved to the more important post of governor of Manisa in western Anatolia. He governs Manisa for about seven years, gaining administrative experience.

On 22 September 1520 his father Selim I dies suddenly at Çorlu while preparing a new campaign. Suleiman is summoned from Manisa and is enthroned in Istanbul on 30 September 1520. He inherits an empire that under his father has just doubled in size by the conquest of Egypt, Syria, the Hijaz, and most of eastern Anatolia.

On 29 August 1521 takes Belgrade, the great Hungarian fortress on the Danube that had repulsed Mehmed II in 1456. Its fall opens the road to central Europe and is celebrated as the great achievement of the new reign.

On 21 December 1522, after a six-month siege, the Knights Hospitaller of Rhodes surrender to Suleiman. The Knights are allowed to evacuate to Malta. Rhodes had been a thorn in the Ottoman side for over 200 years; its capture removes a major Christian stronghold from the eastern Mediterranean.

On 29 August 1526 wins the Battle of Mohacs, annihilating the army of Hungary in a battle lasting less than two hours. King Louis II of Hungary drowns in a marsh while fleeing. The Hungarian crown is shattered. Suleiman enters Buda on 11 September. Hungary will be partitioned between the Ottomans, the Habsburgs, and the vassal principality of Transylvania for the next 150 years.

From 27 September to 14 October 1529 lays siege to Vienna with an army that crossed the Sava on 1 May. The siege fails - the defenders hold the walls and the early winter forces a retreat. It is the first major check on Suleiman's expansion into central Europe.

Launches the first of three great campaigns against the Safavid Persia of Shah Tahmasp I, the Two Iraqs (Iraqayn) campaign of 1534-1535. Takes Tabriz in July 1534 and then Baghdad in November-December 1534, removing Iraq permanently from Persian rule and bringing the shrines of Najaf, Karbala, and Kazimayn under Ottoman protection.

On 27 September 1538 the Ottoman fleet under Hayreddin Barbarossa wins the Battle of Preveza off the western coast of Greece against a Holy League fleet led by the Genoese admiral Andrea Doria. Suleiman is not present in person. The victory makes the Ottomans the leading naval power in the Mediterranean for the next several decades.

In August 1541 occupies Buda outright, ending the legal fiction of an Ottoman vassal kingdom in central Hungary. Buda becomes the seat of an Ottoman pasha and the centre of the new province of Buda. Hungary is now formally part of the empire.

In 1553, on suspicion of plotting with the Janissaries against him, has his eldest and most popular son Sehzade Mustafa strangled in his presence at the imperial camp at Eregli (Konya Eregli) during the eastern campaign of that year. The execution causes deep grief in the army and, in some Ottoman traditions, marks the beginning of the dynasty's moral decline.

Inaugurates the Süleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul, designed by his chief architect Mimar Sinan. It is the great imperial monument of his reign and the most ambitious mosque built in Istanbul since Hagia Sophia. The complex includes madrasas, a hospital, a soup kitchen, and a hospice; Suleiman intends to be buried in its precinct.

In April 1558 his beloved wife and queen Hurrem Sultan (called Roxelana in the West) dies in Istanbul. Hurrem was the first of his slave concubines whom he formally married, an unprecedented act in Ottoman tradition, and his deepest emotional companion for 30 years. He never recovers from her death.

On 1 May 1566, although seriously ill with gout and unable to ride, departs from Istanbul on his thirteenth and final military campaign, against the Habsburgs in Hungary. Has to be carried in a litter for much of the way.

Dies on 6 September 1566 (21 Safar 974 AH) in his tent at the siege of the Hungarian fortress of Szigetvar, which is being heroically defended by the Croatian count Nikola Subic Zrinski. Death precedes the fall of the fortress by less than 36 hours. The grand vizier Sokollu Mehmed Pasha keeps Suleiman's death secret from the army for 48 days, until the new sultan Selim II can reach the army. Suleiman's body is taken back to Istanbul and buried in the turbe (mausoleum) in the precinct of the Süleymaniye Mosque, where his tomb stands today next to that of Hurrem Sultan. His heart and viscera are buried at Szigetvar.
